fact: May 24 is exactly 144 days into the year - that's 12 squared, making it a perfect square day!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically prime time for fireflies to start their magical light shows in backyards and forests.
Cherry trees in Japan are often dropping their petals like pink snow around this time, creating carpets of flowers on the ground.
The sun usually rises before 6 AM in most northern cities by May 24, giving you extra morning light to spot dewdrops sparkling on spider webs.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, May 24 marks the heart of autumn when leaves typically turn brilliant orange and red.
Dandelions are often going completely wild around May 24, transforming from yellow flowers into those white puffball seed heads you can blow apart.
Tadpoles in ponds across North America are typically sprouting their back legs right around now, getting ready to become tiny frogs.
May 24 sits exactly one month before the summer solstice, meaning daylight is growing by about 2 minutes each day in northern places.
Bees are usually busy collecting nectar this time of year - a single bee might visit 5,000 flowers in one day around May 24!
In cold mountain regions, this is often when avalanche lilies push through the last patches of melting snow, creating white and yellow flower explosions.
